behaviors.description: Cache keys generated from identical query parameters are always identical, and keys from different parameters never collide.
behaviors.justification: This is key-derivation logic at a local function/object boundary. The behavior text does not require real DB/network/OS, external contract checks, or model output. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior is explicitly about key generation from parameters (pure transform), not cross-module handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Property-style unit test on the key-builder: same normalized params -> same key; varied params across a large corpus -> distinct keys (within tested domain).

behaviors.description: The TTL for a cache entry is computed correctly based on the data category's configured freshness rule (e.g., user profiles = 5 minutes, product catalog = 1 hour).
behaviors.justification: Failure risk is in wiring between TTL computation and category configuration (multiple modules). No explicit real OS/network/subprocess requirement, and no AI dependence. I considered System, but rejected it because behavior text only states configured freshness mapping and computation, not external contract/round-trip side effect.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test loading real cache config module and TTL service together: for each category, computed TTL equals configured rule; unknown category follows configured fallback.

behaviors.description: When memory usage exceeds the configured threshold, the eviction strategy switches from LRU to LFU as specified in the configuration.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on interaction among memory-usage signal, threshold config, and strategy selector. The text does not explicitly require real OS memory metrics or external contract verification. I considered System, but rejected it because the behavior says 'memory usage exceeds configured threshold' without requiring real process/OS measurement path.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test wires memory-pressure monitor abstraction + config + eviction selector: below threshold -> LRU; above threshold -> LFU; boundary value behavior matches spec.

behaviors.description: A write to the underlying PostgreSQL table invalidates all cache entries whose keys match the affected rows.
behaviors.justification: This is an external round-trip side effect requirement: real DB write must lead to externally visible cache invalidation. That matches SYSTEM TRIAD #3 (prove externally visible side effect via readback) and explicitly references underlying PostgreSQL. Integration with fakes could hide trigger/notification/transaction-order failures.
behaviors.test_description: System test against real PostgreSQL + running cache layer: seed rows and cache entries, perform real SQL write/update, then read through cache path and verify matching keys are invalidated while unrelated keys remain.

behaviors.description: The eviction policy removes the correct entries — least-recently-used under LRU, least-frequently-used under LFU — when the cache is full.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on state handoff across modules (access tracking counters/timestamps + policy engine + capacity enforcement), beyond a single pure function. No explicit real infra contract requirement and no AI dependency. I considered System, but rejected it because behavior text does not require OS/subprocess/network effects or external round-trip contract.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with in-process cache components: drive access patterns to establish recency/frequency, fill to capacity, trigger eviction under LRU and LFU modes, and assert exact victim keys.
